title: C-MAPSS dataset overview
text: The Commercial Modular Aero-Propulsion System Simulation (C-MAPSS) dataset is a public turbofan engine run-to-failure dataset released by NASA Ames Prognostics Center of Excellence. It contains multivariate time series of engine sensor measurements for fleets of simulated engines operated until functional failure. The dataset is split into four subsets (FD001-FD004) that vary in the number of operating conditions and fault modes, and is the most widely used benchmark for remaining useful life (RUL) prognostics research.

title: C-MAPSS subsets FD001-FD004
text: FD001 has one operating condition and one fault mode (HPC degradation) with 100 train and 100 test engines. FD002 has six operating conditions, one fault mode, 260 train and 259 test engines. FD003 has one operating condition but two fault modes (HPC and fan degradation), 100/100 engines. FD004 is the hardest: six conditions, two fault modes, 248 train and 249 test engines. FD001 is the most commonly reported benchmark in papers.

title: C-MAPSS file format
text: Each train/test file is a whitespace-separated table with one row per engine cycle. The 26 columns are: engine_id, cycle number, three operational settings (altitude, Mach, throttle resolver angle), and 21 sensor measurements. Training files contain full run-to-failure trajectories. Test files are truncated at some cycle before failure; the companion RUL_FDxxx.txt file gives the true remaining life for each test engine in engine_id order.

title: Remaining Useful Life (RUL)
text: Remaining Useful Life (RUL) is the number of operating cycles left before an engine reaches a functional failure threshold. For C-MAPSS, the ground-truth RUL of the last training row of an engine is zero; earlier rows have RUL equal to (max_cycle - current_cycle). At test time, one RUL value is predicted per engine, corresponding to its last observed cycle.

title: Piecewise-linear RUL target
text: Training labels are typically clipped with a piecewise-linear function: RUL = min(max_cycle - cycle, R_early), with R_early commonly set to 125 or 130 cycles on FD001. The rationale is that early in an engine's life there is no observable degradation signal, so forcing the model to predict large RULs precisely wastes capacity. Piecewise clipping concentrates learning on the degradation regime near failure.

title: Full list of 21 C-MAPSS sensors
text: The 21 sensor channels are: (1) T2 fan inlet temperature, (2) T24 LPC outlet temperature, (3) T30 HPC outlet temperature, (4) T50 LPT outlet temperature, (5) P2 fan inlet pressure, (6) P15 bypass-duct total pressure, (7) P30 HPC outlet pressure, (8) Nf physical fan speed, (9) Nc physical core speed, (10) epr engine pressure ratio, (11) Ps30 HPC outlet static pressure, (12) phi ratio of fuel flow to Ps30, (13) NRf corrected fan speed, (14) NRc corrected core speed, (15) BPR bypass ratio, (16) farB burner fuel-air ratio, (17) htBleed bleed enthalpy, (18) Nf_dmd demanded fan speed, (19) PCNfR_dmd demanded corrected fan speed, (20) W31 HPT coolant bleed, (21) W32 LPT coolant bleed.

title: Near-constant sensors on FD001
text: On FD001, seven sensor channels carry essentially no information and are routinely dropped before modelling: sensor 1 (T2), sensor 5 (P2), sensor 6 (P15), sensor 10 (epr), sensor 16 (farB), sensor 18 (Nf_dmd), and sensor 19 (PCNfR_dmd). This leaves 14 informative sensors, which is the standard feature set reported in most recent LSTM and CNN papers on FD001.

title: HPC degradation fault mode
text: The primary fault mode in FD001 and FD002 is progressive degradation of the High Pressure Compressor (HPC). As the HPC wears, its isentropic efficiency drops and its flow capacity changes, raising the temperature and pressure required to maintain thrust. Sensors downstream of the HPC — T30, T50, P30, Ps30, and coolant bleeds W31/W32 — exhibit the strongest degradation trends.

title: Fan degradation fault mode
text: FD003 and FD004 add a second concurrent fault mode: progressive fan efficiency loss. Fan degradation disturbs bypass airflow and shows up primarily on sensors measuring bypass ratio (BPR), fan speed (Nf, NRf) and bypass duct pressure (P15). Because test engines in FD003/FD004 can exhibit either or both fault modes, these subsets are harder to model than FD001/FD002.

title: Operating conditions and settings
text: Each row contains three operational settings: altitude (setting 1), Mach number (setting 2), and throttle resolver angle (setting 3). FD001 and FD003 operate at a single condition (sea-level, M ~ 0.25). FD002 and FD004 cycle between six distinct conditions drawn from the cruise/climb/descent envelope. Multi-condition subsets typically require per-condition normalisation to align sensor readings across operating points.

title: Turbofan engine architecture
text: A two-spool high-bypass turbofan consists of: a fan, a Low Pressure Compressor (LPC), a High Pressure Compressor (HPC), a combustor, a High Pressure Turbine (HPT), and a Low Pressure Turbine (LPT). The LP spool links fan and LPC to the LPT; the HP spool links HPC to HPT. C-MAPSS simulates this architecture with thermodynamic component maps, and the 21 sensors are distributed along the gas path between these stages.

title: C-MAPSS asymmetric scoring function
text: The C-MAPSS score penalises late predictions more harshly than early ones because a late prediction corresponds to a missed maintenance window. For each engine with error d = y_pred - y_true, the contribution is exp(-d/13) - 1 if d < 0 (early) and exp(d/10) - 1 if d >= 0 (late). The total score is the sum over all test engines; lower is better. An oracle scores 0; random guessing scores in the tens of thousands on FD001.

title: Standard metrics in C-MAPSS papers
text: Published papers typically report two metrics on the 100 FD001 test engines: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) in cycles, and the asymmetric C-MAPSS score. Some also report Mean Absolute Error (MAE). Strong modern models achieve RMSE in the 11-14 range and scores under 400; the original 2008 challenge winner scored around 512.

title: Babu et al. 2016 baseline
text: Babu et al. introduced a deep convolutional neural network for RUL estimation and reported MLP RMSE 37.56, SVR RMSE 20.96, and CNN RMSE 18.45 on C-MAPSS FD001. Their CNN used 1D convolutions over sensor channels and established CNNs as a strong alternative to hand-crafted features for prognostics.

title: Zheng et al. 2017 LSTM
text: Zheng et al. demonstrated that a two-layer LSTM with piecewise-linear RUL clipping achieves RMSE 16.14 and a C-MAPSS score of 338 on FD001. Their architecture used a window of 30 cycles over the 14 informative sensors and remains the canonical LSTM baseline that newer papers report against.

title: Wu et al. 2018 deep LSTM
source: Wu, Yuan, Dong, Lin. IEEE T. Ind. Inf. 2018
text: Wu et al. proposed a deeper LSTM stack with dropout regularisation and input-level z-score normalisation per engine. They reported RMSE 13.65 and score 280 on FD001, which for several years was the strongest published result for a vanilla LSTM architecture on that subset.

title: Liu et al. 2020 AGCNN
text: Liu et al. introduced an attention-based gated CNN (AGCNN) that combines 1D convolutions with a learned temporal attention mask. AGCNN achieves RMSE 12.42 and C-MAPSS score 226 on FD001, currently among the best published numbers for a purely supervised model of reasonable size.

title: Sliding-window input construction
text: Most recurrent and convolutional models consume a fixed-length window of the last W cycles of an engine. A window of W=30 is typical on FD001, matching the roughly 30-cycle degradation regime. Training is done with sliding windows (stride 1), yielding tens of thousands of samples from only 100 engines. At test time, one window is formed from the last W cycles of each test engine, producing one RUL prediction per engine.

title: Sensor normalisation
text: Two normalisation schemes dominate in the literature. Global min-max scaling to [-1, 1] (fit on training data, reused on test) is the simplest and works well on single-condition subsets like FD001/FD003. For multi-condition subsets (FD002/FD004), per-operating-condition z-score normalisation is preferred because raw sensor scales differ materially between cruise and climb conditions.

title: Early degradation signal
text: Empirically, the first 50-100 cycles of a C-MAPSS engine show no meaningful degradation trend: sensors fluctuate within nominal noise. Useful predictive signal typically emerges 30-50 cycles before failure, which motivates the piecewise-linear RUL cap at 125-130. Models that attempt to predict large RULs precisely tend to overfit to noise.

title: Health Index concept
text: A Health Index (HI) is a scalar in [0, 1] summarising engine condition, where 1 is new-engine health and 0 is failure. HIs can be constructed by fitting a monotone model (e.g. logistic regression or an autoencoder) to sensor data and used as an intermediate target before RUL regression. Not strictly required for C-MAPSS modelling, but useful as a monitoring signal in dashboards.

title: Functional failure threshold
text: C-MAPSS defines functional failure as the point at which simulated engine performance drops below an operational threshold (typically a combination of efficiency and flow-capacity margins). This threshold is the point at which the ground-truth RUL becomes zero. In the real fleet, functional failure does not mean catastrophic failure: it is the latest point at which the engine can still operate within certification margins.

title: Maintenance action: inspect
text: When predicted RUL is moderate (e.g. 40-80 cycles) and there is no strong downward trend, the standard action is a borescope inspection at the next scheduled stop. Inspection defers commitment of spare parts and ground time while collecting additional evidence. Inspection findings can promote the engine to a repair action or demote it back to routine monitoring.

title: Maintenance action: repair
text: When predicted RUL is low (roughly 10-40 cycles) and sensor trends confirm degradation, the recommended action is scheduled repair at the next available maintenance slot. Repair typically targets the degraded module (HPC for FD001-style faults) and restores health without a full overhaul. Cost and downtime are roughly 20-40% of a full replacement.

title: Maintenance action: replace
text: When predicted RUL is very low (under 10 cycles) or the engine has already exceeded a hard safety margin, full module replacement is the safe action. Replacement avoids the risk of in-service failure, which carries severe cost and regulatory consequences. The C-MAPSS asymmetric scoring function formally encodes this asymmetry by heavily penalising late predictions.

title: Prediction uncertainty and ensembles
text: Point predictions of RUL should be accompanied by uncertainty. Common approaches are Monte Carlo dropout at inference, deep ensembles (5-10 models with different seeds), and quantile regression. A 90% prediction interval lets operators choose a conservative (lower-quantile) RUL for safety-critical decisions and a nominal RUL for planning.

title: Transfer between FD subsets
text: Models trained on FD001 often transfer poorly to FD002 or FD004 without adaptation because the operating-condition distributions differ. Successful transfer strategies include condition-aware normalisation, domain-adversarial training, and fine-tuning on a small target-subset slice. Cross-subset transfer remains an active research direction.

title: Streaming inference pattern
text: In production, engines emit new sensor readings every cycle. A streaming RUL service maintains a sliding 30-cycle window per engine and produces a fresh RUL each time a new cycle arrives. The heavy cost is the model forward pass (a few ms on CPU for a small LSTM); preprocessing and windowing cost is negligible.

title: Engine history query tool
text: The Copilot agent exposes one tool, query_engine_history(engine_id), which returns (a) the last 30 cycles of the 14 informative sensors for the requested engine, (b) summary statistics (mean, std, trend) for each sensor, and (c) the most recent RUL prediction from the trained LSTM. The agent uses this tool to ground maintenance recommendations in concrete per-engine evidence before citing retrieved domain knowledge.

title: Why RAG for maintenance decisions
text: A pure point estimate of RUL (e.g. 'engine 42 has 18 cycles left') gives no context for action. Retrieval-augmented generation lets the Copilot pair each recommendation with grounded domain knowledge: the relevant fault mode, the sensors that support it, the maintenance action for the predicted RUL band, and a citation to source documentation. This converts predictions into defensible decisions.

title: Streamlit Copilot UI
text: The demo surface is a Streamlit app deployed as a public Hugging Face Space. The UI has three views: (1) fleet heatmap of predicted RUL per engine, (2) per-engine drill-down with sensor timelines and the model's RUL trajectory, (3) Copilot chat where the agent uses query_engine_history and the RAG index to answer maintenance questions. Cold start is kept under 10 seconds by lazy-loading the LSTM.

title: Hugging Face Spaces deployment
text: The project is deployed as a Streamlit Hugging Face Space. The Space repo contains app.py at the root, a requirements.txt, the serialised LSTM (.keras), the RF model (.joblib), the preprocessing scaler, the FAISS index, and the corpus JSONL. The space is public at huggingface.co/spaces/Ranjithmaddirala/fleetmind and the OpenAI API key is stored as a Space secret.

title: Training configuration used
text: The LSTM in this project is trained with Adam optimiser (initial LR 1e-3), MSE loss, a ReduceLROnPlateau schedule halving LR every 4 epochs without improvement, early stopping on validation RMSE with patience 8, batch size 256, and a 10% validation split. Two stacked LSTM layers (128 and 64 units) with 20% dropout feed a 32-unit dense layer. The model has about 125k parameters and trains on CPU in roughly 13 minutes.

title: Random Forest baseline configuration
text: The Random Forest baseline uses scikit-learn's RandomForestRegressor with 300 trees, max depth 14, and minimum 5 samples per leaf. Features are the 14 raw sensor values plus rolling 30-cycle mean and standard deviation of each sensor, yielding 43 features per row. The RF is trained on the piecewise-clipped RUL target (cap 125) and takes under a minute to train.

title: RMSE vs. C-MAPSS score tradeoffs
text: RMSE rewards unbiased predictions. The C-MAPSS score strongly rewards early (conservative) predictions and punishes late ones. A model optimising pure RMSE often reports a worse C-MAPSS score than a model trained with a tilted loss. In practice, most papers train with MSE or Huber and report both numbers, because retraining with an explicit C-MAPSS-shaped loss gives small RMSE gains but large score improvements.

title: Standard evaluation protocol on FD001
text: The evaluation protocol on FD001 is: fit a scaler on the training engines, train the model on all 100 training engines (or with a held-out validation slice), form a single window from the last 30 cycles of each test engine, predict one RUL per engine, compare to RUL_FD001.txt (100 values in engine_id order), and report RMSE and C-MAPSS score. Predictions are typically clipped to the RUL cap (e.g. 125).

title: Why start with FD001
text: FD001 is the simplest C-MAPSS subset: one operating condition, one fault mode. It is the standard entry point because it isolates the prognostics modelling problem from operating-condition confounds. A model that fails on FD001 has no chance on FD002-FD004. Most architecture-comparison experiments in the literature are reported first (or only) on FD001.

title: Characteristic sensor trends under HPC degradation
text: Under HPC degradation, the most diagnostic trends are: T30 (HPC outlet temperature) rising as efficiency drops, P30 and Ps30 rising as more compression work is needed to meet thrust demand, T50 (LPT outlet temperature) rising because combustor load increases, and W31/W32 (turbine coolant bleeds) rising as the control system tries to protect downstream hot sections. Fan-related sensors stay near-nominal in FD001.

title: Choosing the RUL cap value
text: Common RUL cap values in the literature are 125, 130, and 150 cycles. The choice matters: a cap that is too low collapses useful label variation; a cap that is too high forces the model to predict large values with no signal. On FD001 the typical optimum is between 120 and 135; 125 is the most frequently reported value.

title: Asymmetric loss functions
text: Some papers replace MSE with a directly asymmetric loss that mirrors the C-MAPSS score (e.g. log-cosh variants or custom piecewise exponentials). These losses typically reduce C-MAPSS score by 20-40% at a small RMSE cost, because they explicitly encourage early (conservative) predictions. They require careful clipping to avoid exploding gradients when predictions diverge.

title: Attention and transformer variants
text: Attention mechanisms (AGCNN, self-attention, transformer encoders) typically improve FD001 RMSE by 1-2 cycles over plain LSTM baselines. Transformer-only architectures have been reported with RMSE around 11-12 on FD001. Their gain is mainly on multi-condition subsets (FD002/FD004) where the temporal pattern is more complex than on single-condition FD001.

title: Embedding model used for retrieval
text: The RAG index is built with OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small model, which produces 1536-dimensional embeddings. Corpus embeddings are precomputed and stored in a FAISS IndexFlatIP with L2-normalised vectors, which makes inner-product search equivalent to cosine similarity. Query-time retrieval adds one OpenAI API call per question, which is cheap and sub-200ms.

title: FAISS index choice
text: For a corpus of under a few thousand chunks, IndexFlatIP (exact inner-product search) is the correct choice: it is trivially reproducible, requires no training, and search time is sub-millisecond. Approximate indexes (IVF, HNSW, PQ) become worthwhile only at 100k+ vectors. This project uses IndexFlatIP because the corpus has tens of chunks.

title: Retrieval evaluation metrics
text: Standard retrieval metrics are hit@k (fraction of queries whose expected document appears in the top-k results) and Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR, the mean of 1/rank where rank is the position of the first relevant document, or 0 if not in the top-k). Both are reported on a held-out query set. For this project, hit@1, hit@3, hit@5 and MRR@10 are reported on a 50-pair eval set.

title: Citation format in Copilot answers
text: Every Copilot answer includes one or more citations of the form [doc_id] after the claim they support. Each citation maps to a corpus chunk (title plus source) shown in an expandable UI block. If no retrieved chunk scores above a similarity threshold, the Copilot explicitly says so rather than fabricating a citation, and falls back to the unaugmented RUL prediction alone.

title: Redundancy among informative sensors
text: Among the 14 informative FD001 sensors, several are strongly correlated: T24/T30/T50 all reflect hot-section temperatures, P30/Ps30 both reflect HPC outlet pressure, and Nf/NRf are corrected and raw fan speeds. Principal component analysis shows 3-4 dominant axes of variation. Models do not need hand-chosen dimensionality reduction; modern recurrent and convolutional architectures learn the shared structure end-to-end.

title: Distribution of engine lifetimes on FD001
text: FD001 training engines run between 128 and 362 cycles before failure, with a median around 200. Test engines are truncated at some cycle before failure; the shortest test trajectory is about 30 cycles. The 30-cycle window choice is partly driven by the need to handle the shortest test engines without needing extra padding logic.

title: Copilot agent loop
text: The Copilot runs a minimal agentic loop: (1) receive user question, (2) decide whether to call query_engine_history based on whether an engine is referenced, (3) retrieve top-k corpus chunks for the question, (4) call an OpenAI chat model with the tool output and retrieved context as the system message, (5) return the answer with inline citations. The loop runs at most one tool call per turn to keep latency predictable.

title: Train/validation split strategy
text: Since test labels are held out, validation is done by random shuffle of sliding-window samples with a 10% validation fraction. This is the most common protocol in the FD001 literature. An alternative is engine-level split (hold out k engines entirely), which is more conservative but slightly noisier; reported results are similar.

title: Why not add engineered features
text: Adding engineered features like per-engine regressions, first-difference of sensors, or health indices can nudge FD001 RMSE down by 0.5-1 cycles. The tradeoff is a larger, less transparent feature pipeline to ship. This project keeps the LSTM input as the raw 14 normalised sensors within a 30-cycle window to prioritise simplicity and reproducibility.

title: Variance across training seeds
text: On FD001, LSTM RMSE varies by roughly +/- 0.5-1.0 cycles across random seeds for an otherwise identical training configuration. Papers should report mean and standard deviation across at least 5 runs, though many report a single best run. This project seeds Python, NumPy, and TensorFlow to make the main run reproducible.

title: C-MAPSS release year and PHM08 challenge
text: C-MAPSS was released in 2008 alongside the PHM08 prognostics data challenge. The challenge used a subset of what later became FD001-style data. The FD001-FD004 organisation used in modern papers was standardised in the 2008 release and has not changed, which is why twenty years of papers compare cleanly on the same benchmark.

title: Model input tensor shape
text: The LSTM expects an input tensor of shape (batch, 30, 14): 30 cycles by 14 informative sensors. The RF baseline expects a flat feature vector of length 43 (14 raw + 14 rolling mean + 14 rolling std + 1 cycle index). Both receive data scaled to [-1, 1] by a MinMaxScaler fitted on the training engines.

title: Effect of window length
text: Window lengths in the literature range from 15 to 50 cycles. Shorter windows reduce parameters but lose slow-degradation context; longer windows increase parameters and force left-padding for short test engines. W=30 is the most common choice on FD001 and is used here. Going from W=30 to W=50 typically changes RMSE by under 0.5 cycles.

title: Moving from C-MAPSS to a real fleet
text: A C-MAPSS-trained model cannot be deployed directly to a real engine fleet: sensor layouts, sampling rates, fault distributions, and operating envelopes differ. C-MAPSS is a benchmark for method development. Real deployments require re-curated training data from the target fleet, domain-specific health indices, explicit treatment of missing sensors and calibration drift, and a review loop with human reliability engineers.
